event: Assumption of the Virgin Mary into Heaven
symbols: ascending figure on clouds — bodily assumption into heavenly glory
cherubs and putti supporting Mary — angelic hosts bearing the Mother of God to heaven
upward gaze — orientation toward God and the beatific vision
crown of stars — the woman clothed with the sun from Revelation 12
open arms — receptivity to divine grace and ecstatic joy
divine light from above — the glory of God welcoming Mary
dark blue mantle — heavenly royalty and the depth of faith
description: A Baroque painting showing a woman ascending through billowing clouds into heavenly light. She wears a white blouse with red accents and a voluminous dark blue mantle that swirls dramatically around her. Her face is turned upward toward a radiant light descending from above, and a faint crown or circlet of stars rests on her head. Her arms are open in a gesture of ecstasy and surrender. Several plump cherubs and putti surround her, some supporting her from below, others hovering in the clouds around her.
theological_description: The Assumption of the Virgin depicts Mary's bodily taking up into heavenly glory at the end of her earthly life. While not explicitly narrated in Scripture, the doctrine draws from several biblical foundations. Revelation 12:1 describes 'a woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet, and on her head a crown of twelve stars,' which tradition identifies with Mary in her glorified state. The Assumption is understood as the fulfillment of the Magnificat's promise: 'He has regarded the humble estate of his handmaiden, for behold, from now on all generations will call me blessed' (Luke 1:48). Mary's bodily glorification anticipates the general resurrection promised in 1 Corinthians 15:20-23, where Christ is the firstfruits and those who belong to him will follow. As the new Eve who said 'yes' where the first Eve said 'no,' Mary's assumption represents the full reversal of the Fall — human flesh, once condemned to return to dust (Genesis 3:19), is now taken into the very presence of God. The ascending movement of the composition visually embodies Paul's promise in 1 Thessalonians 4:17 that the faithful will be 'caught up together in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air.'

scripture_refs.reason: The woman clothed with the sun and crowned with stars, traditionally identified with Mary in glory
The Magnificat — Mary's prophetic declaration that all generations will call her blessed, fulfilled in her glorification
Christ as firstfruits of the resurrection, with Mary's assumption as an anticipation of the general resurrection
Arise, O Lord, to your resting place, you and the ark of your might — Mary as the Ark of the New Covenant assumed into God's presence
Being caught up in the clouds to meet the Lord — the eschatological hope visually anticipated in Mary's assumption
